vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters from Same Character Class
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's maxclassrepeat parameter controls requirements for consecutive repeating characters from the same character class. When set to a positive number, it will reject passwords which contain more than that number of consecutive characters from the same character class. Modify the maxclassrepeat setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to equal to prevent a run of ( + 1) or more identical characters.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_maxclassrepeat='' if grep -sq maxclassrepeat /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/maxclassrepeat/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^maxclassrepeat") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_maxclassrepeat" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'settin

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only modifies a PAM password quality parameter (maxclassrepeat) in pwquality.conf, which is a standard STIG hardening step. It does not affect system boot, networking, or critical services and will not lock out existing users.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential impact on password acceptance causing user inconvenience

execution_details.stdout:
ucredit = -1
lcredit = -1
minlen = 8
ocredit = -1
dcredit = -1
dictcheck = 1

 difok = 4
enforce_for_root = 1

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation correctly sets maxclassrepeat to a compliant value and removes duplicate entries, addressing the vulnerability. It is safe and functional, though the script could be refined for safety and idempotency.
concerns: The sed command deletes any line containing maxclassrepeat in all *.conf.d files, including commented lines that might be documentation.
The script assumes the /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d directory exists and contains files; if it doesn't, the glob may expand to the literal pattern and cause errors.
The variable var_password_pam_maxclassrepeat is left empty, making the script harder to adjust to different policy values.
suggested_improvements: Backup pwquality configuration files before editing.
Target only uncommented maxclassrepeat lines when removing duplicates, preserving comments.
Check for the existence of the pwquality.conf.d directory before iterating over it.
Define the desired maxclassrepeat value in a variable and reuse it throughout the script.
Consider using a configuration management tool (e.g., Ansible, Chef) for idempotent state enforcement.
